captions.text: Same answer the single agent gave. Now it’s an opening claim, no longer a verdict.
Cornered by specific objections, the Advocate retreats to a stronger position.

lines.text: Let’s run our architecture question through it. Round one. The Advocate opens with the textbook case for microservices. Independent scaling, team autonomy, fault isolation. The same answer the single agent gave us. But now it’s an opening claim, not a verdict.

The Critic responds to the actual text above it. "You’re assuming an org that can afford this. Nothing says team size. Under twenty engineers, the distributed systems tax eats the autonomy gains. And data consistency? Ignored entirely."

The Pragmatist piles on with operational reality. Kubernetes experience, observability, CI/CD per service. If the team currently ships one Rails app, that’s a year of platform work before the first feature.

Round two is where it gets interesting. The Advocate, cornered by specific objections, retreats to a stronger position: start with a modular monolith. Enforce boundaries in code. Extract a service only when a module proves it needs one. The Critic probes the new position. The Pragmatist answers with concrete mechanisms.

Round three: nobody has anything new. The moderator calls it, and the judge writes the synthesis. Modular monolith, with explicit triggers for extracting services later. Confidence: medium, because team size and ops maturity were never specified. Those unknowns become open questions for the humans.
And notice: that final answer never appeared in the single-agent response. It emerged from the collision. The debate even surfaced its own uncertainty. A single confident answer can’t do that.
One honesty note: this is a cleaned-up good run. Real transcripts are messier. This arc is what you tune your prompts toward.
